verses.text: Asaph calls the people to hear the lessons of the past, resolved 'we will not hide them from their children,' but tell God's praises and works to the coming generation. History is to be taught. Each age owes the next the story of God's deeds.

Asaph calls the people to hear the lessons of the past, resolved 'we will not hide them from their children,' but tell God's praises and works to the coming generation. History is to be taught. Each age owes the next the story of God's deeds.

Asaph calls the people to hear the lessons of the past, resolved 'we will not hide them from their children,' but tell God's praises and works to the coming generation. History is to be taught. Each age owes the next the story of God's deeds.

God set His law in Israel that children yet unborn might 'set their hope in God' and not be like their stubborn fathers. The aim of teaching is faith. We pass on truth so the next generation will trust and obey.

Ephraim, though armed, turned back in battle because they forgot God's works and broke His covenant. Forgetfulness made them fail. To forget what God has done is to lose heart in the day of trial.

He recalls the wonders in Egypt, the parted sea, the guiding cloud and fire, and water gushing from the rock. God's care was lavish. He led, sheltered, and fed His people at every step.
He recalls the wonders in Egypt, the parted sea, the guiding cloud and fire, and water gushing from the rock. God's care was lavish. He led, sheltered, and fed His people at every step.

Yet they sinned still, testing God by demanding food and asking, 'Can God furnish a table in the wilderness?' Unbelief doubted His power. They had seen miracles, yet questioned whether He could provide.

Yet they sinned still, testing God by demanding food and asking, 'Can God furnish a table in the wilderness?' Unbelief doubted His power. They had seen miracles, yet questioned whether He could provide.

Yet they sinned still, testing God by demanding food and asking, 'Can God furnish a table in the wilderness?' Unbelief doubted His power. They had seen miracles, yet questioned whether He could provide.
God was angry at their unbelief, yet rained down manna, 'the corn of heaven,' giving them angels' food in plenty. Mercy met their murmuring. He fed the very people who doubted Him.

He also sent quails like dust, feathered fowl as the sand of the sea, falling about their tents. He granted their craving. God gave them the flesh they demanded.
He also sent quails like dust, feathered fowl as the sand of the sea, falling about their tents. He granted their craving. God gave them the flesh they demanded.
He also sent quails like dust, feathered fowl as the sand of the sea, falling about their tents. He granted their craving. God gave them the flesh they demanded.

They ate and were filled, but while the meat was yet in their mouths God's wrath struck down their strongest. Indulged lust brought judgment. To get our craving against God's will is no true blessing.

For all this they kept sinning, until He slew them; then they sought Him and remembered 'that God was their rock.' Affliction drove them back. Sadly, some only seek God when His hand falls heavy.

Their repentance was hollow, flattering with the mouth, yet God, full of compassion, forgave and restrained His anger, remembering 'that they were but flesh.' His pity outran their falseness. He deals gently with our frailty even when we fail Him.

How often they provoked Him in the desert, forgetting His hand and the day He freed them from the foe. Forgetfulness bred rebellion. A short memory for mercies is a long road to sin.
How often they provoked Him in the desert, forgetting His hand and the day He freed them from the foe. Forgetfulness bred rebellion. A short memory for mercies is a long road to sin.

He recalls the plagues on Egypt — blood, flies, frogs, locusts, and hail on their cattle. God judged the oppressor mightily. The deliverance of His people meant the overthrow of their enemies.

He poured out fierce wrath and smote the firstborn of Egypt, the chief of their strength. Judgment fell at last. The God of patience is also the God of righteous reckoning.
He poured out fierce wrath and smote the firstborn of Egypt, the chief of their strength. Judgment fell at last. The God of patience is also the God of righteous reckoning.
He poured out fierce wrath and smote the firstborn of Egypt, the chief of their strength. Judgment fell at last. The God of patience is also the God of righteous reckoning.

He led His own out 'like sheep,' guided them safely through the wilderness, and brought them to His holy land, casting out the nations before them. He shepherded them home. The God who redeems also settles His people in their inheritance.

Yet there too they tempted and rebelled, turning aside 'like a deceitful bow,' provoking Him with idols. Settled blessing did not cure them. Prosperity proved as great a test as the wilderness.

So God forsook Shiloh, gave His ark into captivity, and let His priests fall by the sword. Judgment reached even the sanctuary. When worship is corrupted, God will remove His glory from it.

Then the Lord 'awaked as one out of sleep,' smote His foes, rejected Ephraim, and chose Judah and Zion, building His sanctuary firm. Mercy returned in His time. After judgment, God established a surer place for His name.

He chose David from the sheepfolds to shepherd His people, who fed them 'according to the integrity of his heart.' A faithful king was given. David's shepherd-care foreshadows Christ, the Good Shepherd of God's flock.
